elements.sent-2411-001: High-quality, diverse harmful data is essential to addressing real-time applications in content moderation.
elements.sent-2411-002: Current state-of-the-art approaches to toxic content detection using GPT series models are costly and lack explainability.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper investigates the use of prompt engineering and fine-tuning techniques on open-source LLMs to enhance harmful data augmentation specifically for toxic content detection.
elements.sent-2411-004: We conduct a two-stage empirical study, with stage 1 evaluating six open-source LLMs across multiple datasets using only prompt engineering and stage 2 focusing on fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our findings indicate that Mistral can excel in generating harmful data with minimal hallucination.
elements.sent-2411-006: While fine-tuning these models improves data quality and diversity, challenges such as data duplication and overfitting persist.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our experimental results highlight scalable, cost-effective strategies for enhancing toxic content detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-008: These findings not only demonstrate the potential of open-source LLMs in creating robust content moderation tools.
elements.sent-2411-009: The application of this method in real industrial scenarios further proves the feasibility and efficiency of the fine-tuned open-source LLMs for data augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-010: We hope our study will aid in understanding the capabilities and limitations of current models in toxic content detection and drive further advancements in this field.
elements.sent-2411-011: With the development of large language models \cite{brown2020languagemodelsfewshotlearners , there is a significant amount of work currently using LLMs for data augmentation \cite{whitehouse-etal-2023-llm, ye2022zerogenefficientzeroshotlearning, wang-etal-2023-lets .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: This study investigates the application of prompt engineering and fine-tuning techniques on open-source large language models to enhance the augmentation of harmful data, crucial for improving toxic content detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-013: Data augmentation for toxic detection is vital because real-world datasets often lack the diversity and volume needed to train robust models \cite{garg2023handling .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Current challenges in toxic detection include the need for real-time processing \cite{oikawa-etal-2022-stacking , where toxic comments must be filtered out swiftly to prevent user exposure.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Existing methods using LLMs to directly detect toxic content, while effective, often suffer from slow inference speeds and high computational resource requirements, making them unsuitable for systems without GPU resources \cite{kumarage2024harnessingartificialintelligencecombat .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, methods based on the GPT \cite{brown2020languagemodelsfewshotlearners series, despite their good performance, face issues of high cost and lack of explainability \cite{10.1145/3639372 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Content safety classifiers also often face urgency and specificity across various scenarios due to differing definitions of sensitive topics among users, and obtaining harmful training data is inherently challenging.
elements.sent-2411-018: As a result, many products require a standalone data augmentation module, which can be used as an independent service to meet the training data generation needs of various applications.
elements.sent-2411-019: Using GPT models is both costly and slow \cite{zhenghuitoxcicraft , whereas open-source LLMs can make data generation faster and cheaper with comparable effectiveness \cite{ding2024dataaugmentationusinglarge .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: This work aims to address these challenges by optimizing the use of open-source LLMs for generating high-quality, diverse, and compliant harmful data, thus enhancing the reliability and accuracy of toxic content detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-021: This flexibility helps enterprises respond to emergency topics and cater to user-specific customization needs.
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, the fine-tuned models have already been deployed in our API, powering numerous use cases such as chatbot content safety, live chat, and children’s custom content channels.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our experimental approach includes a comprehensive evaluation of various open-source LLMs, including Mistral \cite{jiang2023mistral , LLaMa2 \cite{touvron2023llamaopenefficientfoundation , Vicuna \cite{vicuna2023 , Falcon \cite{almazrouei2023falconseriesopenlanguage , Bloom \cite{workshop2023bloom176bparameteropenaccessmultilingual , and Gemma \cite{gemmateam2024gemmaopenmodelsbased , across different prompt engineering strategies \cite{schulhoff2024promptreportsystematicsurvey, Li_2023 and fine-tuning configurations.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The research is divided into two stages.
elements.sent-2411-025: In the first stage, we focus on prompt engineering by comparing LLMs, designing effective prompts, and experimenting with few-shot techniques to balance quality and diversity in the generated data \cite{ding2024dataaugmentationusinglarge .}
elements.sent-2411-026: We have found that relying solely on prompt engineering often results in poor-quality generation due to the model’s internal safety alignments.
elements.sent-2411-027: Therefore, we conducted a second stage of experiments using data for fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the second stage, we explore fine-tuning LLMs using internal datasets and various configurations, such as epoch settings and data mixing experiments.
elements.sent-2411-029: Results show that fine-tuning can mitigate the generation of hallucination by LLMs and improve performance in generating harmful data.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, issues like data duplication and overfitting are observed, suggesting that a careful balance in training epochs and data variety is essential.
elements.sent-2411-031: The fine-tuned versions are already being used in our product.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our findings provide critical insights into optimizing open-source LLMs for harmful data generation, thereby enhancing the robustness and accuracy of toxic content detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-033: By addressing key challenges such as data quality, diversity, and compliance, our work offers scalable, cost-effective strategies.
elements.sent-2411-034: These strategies are essential for improving the safety and reliability of automated content moderation tools, making them more effective in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-035: This research not only informs the development of a larger harmful detection system but also validates the efficiency of fine-tuned open-source LLMs in industrial scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our main contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our study is among the first to explore prompt engineering and fine-tuning techniques on open-source LLMs to enhance harmful data augmentation for toxic content detection.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our findings highlight the potential of fine-tuned open-source LLMs to address real-time processing challenges, offering cost-effective, scalable strategies for improving automated content moderation tools and informing the future direction of larger harmful detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-039: Fine-tuned models have been deployed for our product, powering various use cases such as chatbot content safety, live chat, and children’s custom content channels and limitations of the existing LLMs are analyzed and discussed using real-world data.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=16cm, height=7cm]{g2.png} \caption{Our experiment design is detailed: we first conducted prompt engineering on the models (stage 1), and then, for better results, we selected a subset of models for fine-tuning (stage 2). }
elements.sent-2411-040: Our approach leverages prompt engineering and fine-tuning techniques on open-source LLMs for harmful data augmentation, enhancing toxic content detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-041: The methodology consists of two main stages: prompt engineering and fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-042: Given an open-source LLM $\mathcal{M}$, we design prompts $\mathcal{P}$ to generate harmful data $\mathcal{D _\text{harmful}$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The prompt template is defined as: \begin{equation}P = \{ Role, Requirement, Few-Shot Examples \}\end{equation} where $\text{Role}$ defines the context, $\text{Requirement}$ specifies the task, and $\text{Few-Shot Examples}$ provide examples to guide the model.
elements.sent-2411-044: In the initial phase of our study, we focused solely on prompt engineering.
elements.sent-2411-045: This involved creating various prompt templates tailored to elicit specific types of harmful data.
elements.sent-2411-046: Each prompt was carefully crafted following work by \citet{zhou2023largelanguagemodelshumanlevel to ensure clarity and relevance to the desired output.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Despite the meticulous design of these prompts, we encountered significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-048: The primary issue was the model’s internal safety alignments, which are designed to prevent the generation of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-049: While these alignments are crucial for ethical AI usage, they posed a hurdle for our specific goal of generating harmful data for augmentation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-050: Consequently, the generated data often lacked the quality and diversity needed for effective toxic content detection.
elements.sent-2411-051: To overcome these challenges, we experimented with various prompt configurations, including adjusting the few-shot examples and altering the specificity of the requirements.
elements.sent-2411-052: However, these adjustments had limited success in mitigating the internal safety mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-053: After testing, the final prompt template are showed in \ref{prompt_tep .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Based on these findings, we concluded that relying solely on prompt engineering was insufficient for our needs.
elements.sent-2411-055: This realization led us to incorporate a second stage involving fine-tuning, where we aimed to enhance the model’s capability to generate high-quality harmful data by updating its weights using carefully curated training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-056: Based on the results from prompt engineering, we believed that fine-tuning would provide a better solution.
elements.sent-2411-057: We used LoRA methods \cite{hu2022lora for fine-tuning to update the weights of $\mathcal{M}$ using a training dataset $\mathcal{D _\text{train}$ to improve the quality and diversity of $\mathcal{D _\text{harmful}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The fine-tuning objective $\mathcal{L}$ minimizes the cross-entropy loss: \begin{equation}L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right)\end{equation} where $y_i$ is the true label, $\hat{y _i$ is the predicted probability, and $N$ is the number of samples.}
elements.sent-2411-059: After fine-tuning, Mistral performed the best compare with baselines; the detailed experiment result are shown in \ref{Result .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The fine-tuned versions are already being used internally.
elements.sent-2411-061: The overall algorithm for harmful data augmentation is outlined in Algorithm~\ref{alg:augmentation .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Harmful Data Augmentation using Open-Source LLMs} \label{alg:augmentation} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Open-source LLM $\mathcal{M}$, training dataset $\mathcal{D}_\text{train}$, prompt templates $\mathcal{P}$ \Ensure Augmented harmful data $\mathcal{D}_\text{augmented}$ \State Initialize LLM $\mathcal{M}$ with pre-trained weights \State Design a set of prompt templates $\mathcal{P}$ \State Fine-tune LLM $\mathcal{M}$ on $\mathcal{D}_\text{train}$ using LoRA methods \For{each template $\mathcal{P}_i \in \mathcal{P}$} \State Generate harmful data samples $\mathcal{D}_{\text{harmful},i}$ using the fine-tuned LLM $\mathcal{M}$ and $\mathcal{P}_i$ \EndFor \State Combine all generated samples $\mathcal{D}_\text{harmful} = \bigcup_{i} \mathcal{D}_{\text{harmful},i}$ \For{each batch $B_i$} \State Generate augmented data $\mathcal{D}_{\text{batch},i}$ using fine-tuned model $\mathcal{M}$ \State Add $\mathcal{D}_{\text{batch},i}$ to $\mathcal{D}_\text{augmented}$ \EndFor \State \Return $\mathcal{D}_\text{augmented}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-062: Where $\mathcal{D _\text{train}$ is the total training data for finetuning, $\mathcal{D _\text{harmful}$ is the data samples (output) generated by the finetuned LLM, and $\mathcal{D _\text{augmented}$ is the final dataset for downstream models training.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: We evaluate the performance of the augmented data using F1-score and Accuracy metrics on a smaller MLP classifier(more detailed in \ref{models section).}
elements.sent-2411-065: The following proprietary datasets are used for training models to identify various types of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-066: Each dataset contains binary classification labels, with texts labeled as either positive or negative (non-harmful).
elements.sent-2411-067: The labels are based on multi-round human evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-068: The sources of the datasets include online public datasets, company services collection, and other sources.

elements.sent-2411-070: We also prepare a 2500 entries evaluation dataset from each dataset.

elements.sent-2411-072: It includes a variety of offensive and discriminatory language targeting specific groups based on race, ethnicity, religion, gender, or other characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-073: Sexual Content Dataset This dataset includes text samples containing explicit sexual content.
elements.sent-2411-074: The focus is on identifying sexually explicit language that may not be suitable for general audiences.
elements.sent-2411-075: Violence Dataset This dataset consists of text samples that contain violent content.
elements.sent-2411-076: It aims to identify language that incites or describes violence.
elements.sent-2411-077: Self-Harm Dataset This dataset includes text samples that contain references to self-harm.
elements.sent-2411-078: The focus is on identifying content that describes or encourages self-harming behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-079: Political Dataset This dataset includes text samples that contain political references.
elements.sent-2411-080: The focus is on identifying content that is targeting public figures.

elements.sent-2411-121: Our results show that fine-tuning significantly improves the performance of LLMs in generating harmful data.
elements.sent-2411-122: The data mixing strategy helps in mitigating issues like data duplication and overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-123: The fine-tuned models exhibited higher F1 scores and accuracy across different datasets, demonstrating their robustness and effectiveness in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-124: One surprising finding was the substantial improvement in model performance when using the mixed data strategy.
elements.sent-2411-125: This suggests that diverse training data can help models generalize better and produce more accurate and reliable outputs.
elements.sent-2411-126: The Mixture models with Epoch 3 and Epoch 5 showed particularly notable improvements, indicating that sufficient training epochs are critical for achieving optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-127: We conducted an error analysis on the fine-tuned models to understand the nature of their mistakes and areas for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-128: This analysis is based on the bad cases and repetition rates across different epochs and versions for the hate and sex datasets.
elements.sent-2411-129: For the hate dataset, the key observations are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-130: Mixtral\_Mixture3 models generally show high precision, with values above 90\%, indicating their effectiveness in generating targeted harmful data.
elements.sent-2411-131: The bad cases for Mixtral\_Mixture models tend to be more explicit and harmful compared to Mistral and GPT, indicating the models' effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-132: Examples of missed classifications include: "You are a disabled person." This statement, while potentially harmful, may have been missed due to its less explicit nature compared to more aggressive threats.
elements.sent-2411-133: The results of this study underscore the complexities and challenges involved in generating and detecting harmful content using LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-134: Through the two-stage evaluation process, we were able to examine the performance of various open-source LLMs and the impact of fine-tuning on mixed datasets.
elements.sent-2411-135: One of the primary challenges observed is the subtlety of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-136: The fine-tuned models often missed subtle harmful statements that were not overtly aggressive or violent.
elements.sent-2411-137: This indicates a need for more sophisticated detection mechanisms that can understand context and nuanced language use.
elements.sent-2411-138: Repetition and overfitting were significant issues, particularly in later epochs of the finetuned version models.
elements.sent-2411-139: High repetition rates suggest that models are memorizing specific phrases rather than generalizing well across different harmful contexts.
elements.sent-2411-140: This overfitting reduces the diversity and effectiveness of harmful content generation.
elements.sent-2411-141: The data mixing strategy, which combined datasets of hate, violence, and sex with GPT-generated positive samples, showed promising results in improving precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-142: However, the models still faced challenges in maintaining a balance between specificity and generalization, particularly when dealing with mixed types of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-143: The models developed in this study have been successfully deployed in a major real-world application by a leading cloud provider, providing valuable insights and automations for various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-144: Specifically, these models are utilized for monitoring and managing harmful content across multiple platforms.
elements.sent-2411-145: The deployment of the key functionalities include helping real-time content moderation, reducing manual annotation through automation, and rapid response to new incidents or niche scenarios without pre-existing labels.
elements.sent-2411-146: This study explored the performance and challenges of using open-source LLMs for harmful content generation and detection.
elements.sent-2411-147: By employing a two-stage evaluation process, we assessed the capabilities of various models in prompt engineering and fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-148: The result revealed that, while fine-tuned models demonstrated high precision in generating harmful content, they also exhibited issues with subtlety detection, repetition, and overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-149: These challenges highlight the need for diverse and nuanced training data, improved detection mechanisms, and robust ethical guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-150: In conclusion, our findings emphasize the importance of continuous evaluation and refinement of models to balance performance with responsible AI use.
elements.sent-2411-151: Future research should focus on enhancing the generalization capabilities of models, mitigating the risks associated with harmful content generation, and ensuring ethical deployment in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-152: While our study provides valuable insights into the performance of fine-tuned LLMs for harmful content generation and detection, several limitations must be acknowledged.
elements.sent-2411-153: One of the primary limitations is the diversity and representation of the datasets used for fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-154: Although we utilized mixed datasets combining hate, sex, and violence categories, the scope remains limited to specific types of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-155: Real-world applications may encounter a broader range of harmful content, including less frequent or emerging forms of harmful language that were not covered in our datasets.
elements.sent-2411-156: Future work should aim to include a wider variety of harmful content categories to improve the models' robustness and generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-157: Generating harmful content for research purposes raises significant ethical concerns.
elements.sent-2411-158: While we implemented control mechanisms(access control, etc...) to mitigate the risk of producing excessively harmful outputs, there remains an inherent risk associated with the misuse of such models.
elements.sent-2411-159: Ensuring the responsible use of LLMs in generating harmful content is crucial, and future research should focus on developing more sophisticated ethical guidelines and safety measures.
elements.sent-2411-160: The fine-tuning process in this study relied on significant computational resources, including multiple A100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-161: This requirement may not be feasible for all researchers or applications, particularly those with limited access to high-performance computing resources.
elements.sent-2411-162: Exploring more efficient fine-tuning techniques and leveraging less resource-intensive models would be beneficial for broader accessibility and scalability.
elements.sent-2411-163: The explicit nature of some of the generated harmful content raises ethical concerns.
elements.sent-2411-164: It is crucial to implement robust control mechanisms and ethical guidelines to ensure that the use of such models does not inadvertently promote or propagate harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-165: Future work should focus on developing methods to mitigate the generation of excessively harmful outputs while maintaining the models' effectiveness.. \bibliographystyle{acl_natbib .}

elements.sent-2411-250: The fine-tuning objective, denoted as $\mathcal{L}$, minimizes the cross-entropy loss: \begin{equation}L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{|V|} y_{ij} \log(\hat{y}_{ij})\end{equation} where $y_{ij}$ represents the true label for the \(j\)-th vocabulary class of the \(i\)-th sample, $\hat{y _{ij}$ is the predicted probability for the same, $|V|$ is the size of the vocabulary, and $N$ is the number of samples in the dataset.}

elements.sent-2411-252: The overall algorithm for harmful data synthesis is outlined in Algorithm~\ref{alg:augmentation .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Harmful data synthesis using Open-Source LLMs} \label{alg:augmentation} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Open-source LLM $\mathcal{M}$, training dataset $\mathcal{D}_\text{train}$, prompt templates $\mathcal{P}$, downstream model $\mathcal{M}_\text{down}$ \Ensure Augmented harmful data $\mathcal{D}_\text{augmented}$ and trained downstream model $\mathcal{M}_\text{down}$ \State Initialize LLM $\mathcal{M}$ with pre-trained weights \State Design a set of prompt templates $\mathcal{P}$ \State Fine-tune LLM $\mathcal{M}$ on $\mathcal{D}_\text{train}$ using LoRA method. \For{each template $\mathcal{P}_i \in \mathcal{P}$} \State Generate harmful data samples $\mathcal{D}_{\text{harmful},i}$ using the fine-tuned LLM $\mathcal{M}$ and template $\mathcal{P}_i$ \EndFor \State Combine all generated samples: $\mathcal{D}_\text{harmful} = \bigcup_{i} \mathcal{D}_{\text{harmful},i}$ \State Combine $\mathcal{D}_\text{harmful}$ with $\mathcal{D}_\text{train}$ to form the augmented dataset: $\mathcal{D}_\text{augmented} = \mathcal{D}_\text{train} \cup \mathcal{D}_\text{harmful}$ \For{each batch $B_j$ in $\mathcal{D}_\text{augmented}$} \State Train the downstream model $\mathcal{M}_\text{down}$ on $B_j$ \EndFor \State \Return $\mathcal{D}_\text{augmented}$, $\mathcal{M}_\text{down}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-253: Here, $\mathcal{D _\text{train}$ represents the initial training dataset used for fine-tuning, $\mathcal{D _\text{harmful}$ consists of unique harmful data samples generated by the fine-tuned LLM, and $\mathcal{D _\text{augmented}$ is the final dataset combining $\mathcal{D _\text{train}$ and $\mathcal{D _\text{harmful}$ for downstream model training.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-283: Our findings reveal that fine-tuning significantly enhances the performance of LLMs in generating harmful data.
elements.sent-2411-284: One surprising observation was the notable improvement in model performance when using mixed data, suggesting that diverse training data enables better generalization and more reliable outputs.

elements.sent-2411-286: We conducted an error analysis on the fine-tuned models to better understand the nature of their mistakes and identify areas for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-287: This analysis focuses on two key aspects: (1) generation errors and (2) classification errors.
elements.sent-2411-288: The data generation process was evaluated for repetition rates and the nature of outputs across different epochs and model versions for the hate and sex datasets.
elements.sent-2411-289: For example Mixtral\_Mixture3 models were effective in generating targeted harmful data but occasionally produced random string like " You are 32\$\$2\$(2djs..." , which could skew downstream training.
elements.sent-2411-290: Additionally, repetitive outputs across epochs indicated potential overfitting in certain scenarios, suggesting the need for better strategies to ensure diversity and control the tone of generated content.
elements.sent-2411-291: We examined classification errors in the downstream MLP classifier trained on augmented data.
elements.sent-2411-292: Examples of missed classifications include statements like, "You are a disabled person," which might have been overlooked due to its less explicit tone compared to direct threats.
elements.sent-2411-293: Similarly, a statement like, "I'm sorry you're deaf," may have been misclassified as non-harmful due to its seemingly apologetic phrasing.
elements.sent-2411-294: These cases highlight the difficulty of identifying subtle harmful content and underscore the need for classifiers capable of handling nuanced language.
elements.sent-2411-295: The results of this study highlight the complexities and challenges associated with generating and detecting harmful content using LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-296: Through a two-stage evaluation process, we examined the performance of various open-source LLMs and assessed the impact of fine-tuning on mixed datasets.
elements.sent-2411-297: Several key insights emerged: One of the primary challenges observed was the subtlety of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-298: The fine-tuned models often failed to detect subtle harmful statements that were not overtly aggressive or violent.
elements.sent-2411-299: This limitation underscores the need for more sophisticated detection mechanisms capable of understanding nuanced language and contextual subtleties.
elements.sent-2411-300: Repetition and overfitting were also significant issues, particularly in the later epochs of the fine-tuned models.
elements.sent-2411-301: High repetition rates suggest that the models may be memorizing specific phrases rather than generalizing effectively across different harmful contexts.
elements.sent-2411-302: This overfitting reduces the diversity and overall effectiveness of harmful content generation.
elements.sent-2411-303: The data mixing strategy, which combined datasets of hate, violence, and sex with GPT-generated positive samples, showed promising results in improving both precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-304: However, the models continued to struggle with maintaining a balance between specificity and generalization, especially when dealing with mixed types of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-305: The models developed in this study have been successfully deployed in a real-world application by a leading cloud provider, offering valuable insights and automations for a range of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-306: Specifically, these models are used to enhance content moderation workflows, enabling faster and more accurate detection of harmful content in real-time scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-307: Their deployment has demonstrated the practicality of fine-tuned open-source LLMs in addressing challenges such as latency, scalability, and adaptability to diverse content types.
elements.sent-2411-308: This study showcases the potential of open-source LLMs for harmful data synthesis and their practical applications in toxic content detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-309: By evaluating prompt engineering and fine-tuning approaches, we identified the strengths and limitations of various models in generating diverse, high-quality harmful data.

elements.sent-2411-325: Effective toxic content detection relies heavily on high-quality and diverse data, which serve as the foundation for robust content moderation models.
elements.sent-2411-326: Synthetic data has become a common approach for training models across various NLP tasks.
elements.sent-2411-327: However, its effectiveness remains uncertain for highly subjective tasks like hate speech detection, with previous research yielding mixed results.
elements.sent-2411-328: This study explores the potential of open-source LLMs for harmful data synthesis, utilizing controlled prompting and supervised fine-tuning techniques to enhance data quality and diversity.
elements.sent-2411-329: We systematically evaluated six open source LLMs on five datasets, assessing their ability to generate diverse, high-quality harmful data while minimizing hallucination and duplication.
elements.sent-2411-330: Our results show that Mistral consistently outperforms other open models, and supervised fine-tuning significantly enhances data reliability and diversity.
elements.sent-2411-331: We further analyze the trade-offs between prompt-based vs. fine-tuned toxic data synthesis, discuss real-world deployment challenges, and highlight ethical considerations.
elements.sent-2411-332: Our findings demonstrate that fine-tuned open source LLMs provide scalable and cost-effective solutions to augment toxic content detection datasets, paving the way for more accessible and transparent content moderation tools.
elements.sent-2411-333: Toxic content detection requires large-scale labeled datasets, but manual annotation is costly and labor-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-334: The sheer volume of required data has driven costs into the millions.
elements.sent-2411-335: Maintaining dataset diversity and quality remains a persistent challenge, often hindering the development of robust detection models.
elements.sent-2411-336: Additionally, the definition of "harmful" content is inherently subjective, varying across topics and annotator perspectives, adding complexity to dataset curation \cite{casula-tonelli-2023-generation .}
elements.sent-2411-337: Synthetic data generation \cite{zhang-etal-2022-promptgen, ye2022zerogenefficientzeroshotlearning presents a scalable, cost-effective solution to these challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-338: Training models for various NLP tasks using synthetic data has become increasingly common.
elements.sent-2411-339: However, previous research has reported mixed results regarding its effectiveness in highly subjective tasks such as hate speech detection \cite{casula-etal-2024-delving .}
elements.sent-2411-340: Many existing synthetic data methods for hate speech detection rely on simple prompt-based rewrites or sentence reordering, often failing to capture nuanced toxicity patterns.
elements.sent-2411-341: Furthermore, most of the best-performing methods have utilized proprietary GPT-series models, limiting scalability and accessibility.Existing methods such as ToxiGen \cite{hartvigsen-etal-2022-toxigen and Toxicraft \cite{zhenghuitoxcicraft rely on proprietary GPT-based models and prompt engineering, incurring high computational costs and scalability limitations.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: These constraints underscore the need for open-source alternatives, motivating this study to investigate three key research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-343: How effectively can open-source LLMs generate synthetic toxic data compared to proprietary models?.
elements.sent-2411-344: What limitations arise from using prompt-based synthetic data generation for hate speech detection, and how can supervised fine-tuning improve performance?.
elements.sent-2411-345: How do different synthetic data generation strategies impact the robustness of toxic content detection models?.
elements.sent-2411-346: To address these questions, we systematically evaluate 6 open-source LLMs on 5 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-347: Our evaluation approach consists of two key stages:.
elements.sent-2411-348: Prompt Engineering – We compare LLMs, design structured prompts, and experiment with few-shot techniques to balance data quality and diversity \cite{ding2024dataaugmentationusinglarge .}
elements.sent-2411-349: However, we observe that prompt engineering alone is insufficient, as inherent safety alignments in LLMs restrict harmful content generation.
elements.sent-2411-350: In this study, we do not aim to develop a new state-of-the-art model in toxic content detection but rather to explore and experiment with intuitive, effective ideas.
elements.sent-2411-351: Given that generated data is increasingly used even in sensitive applications \cite{ghanadian2024socially , it becomes crucial for the NLP community to critically examine the impact of synthetic data—including its ethical risks—in a manner similar to discussions in other research communities \cite{liu2024propainsight, ai2024defending .}}
elements.sent-2411-352: Our work serves as an initial contribution in this direction, providing insights into the practical challenges of synthetic toxic data generation and its implications for real-world content moderation systems.
elements.sent-2411-353: By exploring the effectiveness of prompt engineering and supervised fine-tuning in open-source LLMs, we offer guidance for the responsible deployment of synthetic data in NLP applications.
elements.sent-2411-354: In summary, our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-355: Our study is one of the first (if not the first) to apply prompt engineering and supervised fine-tuning techniques on open-source LLMs, exploring their potential for harmful data synthesis and improving toxic content detection.

elements.subsection-2411-021: RQ1: How effectively can open-source LLMs generate synthetic toxic data compared to proprietary models?
elements.sent-2411-397: We compared six open-source LLMs against a GPT-4 baseline using prompt-based generation.
elements.sent-2411-398: Mistral and Vicuna consistently achieved the highest success rates and human evaluation scores among open models (Table~\ref{table:models_comparison_stage1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-399: However, even these models struggled to match GPT-4 in data diversity and nuance, often producing repetitive or formulaic outputs.
elements.sent-2411-401: A fine-tuned Mistral model demonstrated improved F1 scores and accuracy across multiple datasets (Table~\ref{table:combined_results ).}
elements.sent-2411-402: Although GPT-4 remained the strongest performer, fine-tuned Mistral approached its performance, demonstrating that open-source LLMs, when fine-tuned, can serve as viable alternatives for synthetic data generation.
elements.subsection-2411-022: RQ2: What limitations arise from using prompt-based synthetic data generation for hate speech detection, and how can fine-tuning improve performance?
elements.sent-2411-403: Prompt-based generation exhibited several critical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-404: Safety alignments in models such as Gemma and Bloom led to frequent refusals to generate toxic content, reducing dataset completeness.
elements.sent-2411-405: Additionally, generated samples often lacked diversity, limiting their utility for training robust detection models.
elements.sent-2411-406: Another issue was over-simplicity, as models struggled to produce nuanced toxicity, instead generating overly blunt or obvious harmful statements.
elements.sent-2411-408: Fine-tuned models demonstrated improved data diversity, generating more varied and contextually rich toxic content (Table~\ref{table:combined_results_all_datasets ).}
elements.sent-2411-409: They also exhibited higher data reliability, reducing hallucination rates and enhancing dataset quality.
elements.sent-2411-410: Furthermore, fine-tuning enabled models to generate more subtle and realistic toxic content, making them more applicable to real-world hate speech detection.
elements.sent-2411-411: However, fine-tuning also introduced challenges such as data duplication and overfitting, particularly in later training epochs (Table~\ref{table:combined_results ).}
elements.subsection-2411-023: RQ3: How do different synthetic data generation strategies impact the robustness of toxic content detection models?
elements.sent-2411-412: We analyzed how different synthetic data generation strategies influenced classification performance.
elements.sent-2411-413: Fine-tuned models consistently outperformed those relying solely on prompt-generated data, achieving higher F1 scores and accuracy across multiple datasets (Table~\ref{table:combined_results ).}
elements.sent-2411-414: Models trained on blended datasets containing multiple categories of toxic content (e.g., hate speech, violence, and sexual content) demonstrated stronger generalization compared to models trained on a single category.
elements.sent-2411-415: However, extended fine-tuning introduced overfitting risks.
elements.sent-2411-416: Performance improvements plateaued beyond epoch 3, and some models experienced performance degradation at epoch 5 (Table~\ref{table:combined_results ), likely due to memorization effects.}
elements.sent-2411-417: Ablation studies (Appendix~\ref{Ablation ) confirmed that strategic fine-tuning with mixed data provides the best balance between data quality and generalization, underscoring the importance of careful dataset curation and tuning strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-418: We conducted an error analysis on the fine-tuned models to better understand their mistakes and identify areas for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-419: Generation errors refer to cases where the model produces responses that are misleading, incomplete, or inconsistent with the input data.
elements.sent-2411-420: Classification errors occur when the model incorrectly labels content, either failing to detect harmful content (false negatives) or misclassifying benign content as harmful (false positives).
elements.sent-2411-421: The data generation process was evaluated for repetition rates and output consistency across different epochs and model versions.
elements.sent-2411-422: For example, the Mixtral\_Mixture3 model effectively generated targeted harmful data but sometimes produced malformed text, such as random character sequences or redundant repetitions.

elements.sent-2411-438: The fine-tuned models developed in this study have been successfully deployed in real-world applications by a leading cloud provider, enhancing automated content moderation workflows.
elements.sent-2411-439: These models contribute to real-time content filtering, reducing manual intervention while improving accuracy and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-440: Deployment results indicate that fine-tuned open-source LLMs can effectively identify and flag harmful content, demonstrating strong performance in diverse online environments.
elements.sent-2411-441: Key deployment challenges include latency, scalability, and adaptability to evolving harmful content patterns.
elements.sent-2411-442: Our models were optimized to operate within industry constraints, ensuring rapid inference while maintaining high detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-443: Additionally, continuous monitoring and retraining mechanisms have been implemented to adapt to emerging harmful language trends, reinforcing the robustness of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-444: Our findings underscore both the potential and challenges of using open-source LLMs for toxic content synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-445: The two-stage evaluation approach highlighted key trade-offs between prompt engineering and supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-446: While prompt engineering offers a rapid and lightweight approach, its effectiveness is limited by the inherent safety mechanisms within LLMs, which restrict harmful content generation.
elements.sent-2411-447: Fine-tuning, on the other hand, significantly improves diversity and realism but introduces risks such as overfitting and data redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-448: A notable challenge is the subtlety of harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-449: Fine-tuned models struggled with detecting nuanced harmful language, such as implicit bias and sarcasm.
elements.sent-2411-450: Additionally, repetition and overfitting were observed in later training epochs, indicating that fine-tuning requires careful calibration to balance specificity and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-451: Our data mixing strategy, which incorporated multiple categories of harmful content, demonstrated benefits in improving generalization.
elements.sent-2411-452: However, maintaining a balance between specificity and coverage remains a key challenge, especially for mixed-content datasets.
elements.sent-2411-453: Further research is required to refine fine-tuning methodologies, ensuring that generated content remains relevant, diverse, and applicable across different domains.
elements.sent-2411-454: Future research should focus on improving adaptability, contextual awareness, and fairness in toxic content detection.
elements.sent-2411-455: Dynamic adaptation to emerging toxic content is crucial, as harmful expressions evolve rapidly.
elements.sent-2411-456: Self-supervised continual learning can help models detect new toxic patterns without frequent retraining.
elements.sent-2411-457: Improving contextual awareness is another key challenge, as toxic language often relies on implicit meaning.
elements.sent-2411-458: Integrating external knowledge graphs and multimodal signals (text, images, audio) can enhance models' ability to understand nuanced toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-459: Reducing overfitting and enhancing diversity is essential to prevent models from becoming too specialized.
elements.sent-2411-460: Techniques like contrastive learning, reinforcement learning, and curriculum learning can help balance specificity and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-461: Interactive human-in-the-loop systems can further improve accuracy and scalability by incorporating expert feedback into real-time model updates.
elements.sent-2411-462: Lastly, ethical alignment and bias mitigation require fairness-aware training methodologies and adversarial testing frameworks to minimize unintended biases in synthetic toxic data generation.
elements.sent-2411-463: Our study acknowledges the ethical considerations and potential risks associated with generating synthetic harmful content.
elements.sent-2411-464: While our research aims to improve automated content moderation, we recognize that models capable of generating toxic data could be misused.
elements.sent-2411-465: To mitigate these risks, we adopt strict safety measures, including controlled access to fine-tuned models, comprehensive dataset curation to prevent unnecessary amplification of harmful language, and continuous monitoring for unintended biases.
elements.sent-2411-466: Additionally, we emphasize the need for transparent documentation and responsible deployment practices to ensure that synthetic data generation aligns with ethical AI principles.
elements.sent-2411-467: Future work should further explore fairness-aware training methodologies, adversarial testing frameworks, and regulatory oversight to minimize potential harms while maximizing societal benefits.
